parts.content: Describe how mental toughness affects performance during adversity.
parts.markscheme:
- Mentally tough athletes maintain focus on relevant cues despite setbacks or distractions <marks>A1</marks>
- They exhibit reduced negative emotional responses (such as frustration or anxiety) <marks>A1</marks>
- They demonstrate the ability to recover quickly from mistakes and maintain consistency <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Explain how goal-setting contributes to mental toughness.
parts.markscheme:
- Provides a clear structure and direction for training and competition <marks>A1</marks>
- Enhances motivation and persistence by breaking down long-term objectives into manageable tasks <marks>A1</marks>
- Successfully achieving short-term goals builds self-confidence and self-efficacy <marks>A1</marks>
- Helps an athlete maintain long-term focus during periods of hardship or low progress <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Outline how self-talk can influence confidence and focus in athletes.
parts.markscheme:
- Positive self-talk builds self-belief and encourages a positive mindset <marks>A1</marks>
- It helps reduce self-doubt and mitigates the impact of performance anxiety <marks>A1</marks>
- Instructional self-talk refocuses attention on technical execution and relevant performance cues <marks>A1</marks>
- It encourages a sense of calm and self-control during high-stress situations <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Discuss how coaches can help athletes develop mental toughness over time.
parts.markscheme:
- Utilize simulations and pressure drills that mimic the stressors of competition <marks>A1</marks>
- Reinforce resilience by providing constructive feedback and support during difficult training phases <marks>A1</marks>
- Promote reflection and self-analysis after failures to facilitate learning and emotional growth <marks>A1</marks>
- Explicitly teach mental skills such as visualization, self-talk, and relaxation techniques <marks>A1</marks>
- Foster a growth mindset and encourage athlete accountability for their own preparation and performance <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

questions.specification: Why is personality important for athletes’ behaviour in training and competition?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks why personality is important for athletes, and the correct idea is that personality influences consistent behaviours in training and competition—matching how personality traits affect thinking, behavior, and performance.

options.content: It can have limited impact compared with physical and technical factors.
It influences consistent behaviours in training and competition.
It mainly affects motivation rather than behaviour during performance.
It determines physical fitness outcomes regardless of training.

questions.specification: A coach praises a player for demonstrating teamwork. According to Social Learning Theory, this is an example of:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.2 Social learning theory and its role in shaping behavior through observation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.2 Social learning theory and its role in shaping behavior through observation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario involves a coach praising teamwork to encourage the behavior; in Social Learning Theory this is reinforcement (vicarious/direct) shaping behavior, which is covered under social learning theory shaping behavior through observation and reinforcement.

options.content: Positive reinforcement shaping behaviour
options.markscheme: Social Learning Theory (SLT) proposes that behaviour is influenced by environmental factors such as reinforcement. Praise from a coach acts as positive reinforcement, which increases the likelihood of the behaviour being repeated.

options.content: Genetic predisposition
options.markscheme: Genetic predisposition is associated with biological or trait-based theories of personality, which argue that behaviour is innate rather than learned through social interaction.

options.content: Personality being fixed
options.markscheme: The idea that personality is fixed is a core tenet of trait theory. Social Learning Theory, conversely, suggests that behaviour is dynamic and can change based on social context and learning.

options.content: Cognitive restructuring
options.markscheme: Cognitive restructuring is a therapeutic process used to identify and challenge irrational or maladaptive thoughts; it is not the primary mechanism described in this scenario.

questions.specification: Over a 10-year career, an elite swimmer becomes more confident and resilient primarily due to repeated reinforcement and modelling within the sport environment. This change in personality is best explained by:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.3 Long-term changes in personality through experience and reflection (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.3 Long-term changes in personality through experience and reflection (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The stem describes an athlete becoming more confident and resilient across a 10-year career, which aligns with long-term personality change through experience and reflection (personality development over time).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.2 Social learning theory and its role in shaping behavior through observation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.2 Social learning theory and its role in shaping behavior through observation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: One option is social learning theory; observational learning and environmental influences can shape behavior/personality, though the best match in the stem is explicit long-term change over time.

options.content: Genetic inheritance
Social learning theory
options.markscheme: Best fits the stem because confidence and resilience can be learned over time through reinforcement, observation of role models, and feedback in the sporting environment.

options.content: Cognitive dissonance
options.markscheme: Cognitive dissonance explains resolving inconsistency between attitudes and behaviour, not gradual development of confidence/resilience over a career.

options.content: Trait theory (personality is stable over time)
options.markscheme: Trait theory emphasises relative stability of traits, so it does not best explain a within-person increase in confidence and resilience across 10 years.

questions.specification: Why does the idea of personality change challenge fixed-trait models?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.3 Long-term changes in personality through experience and reflection (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question focuses on how personality can change over time through life experiences, which directly challenges fixed-trait (static) models—this matches long-term changes in personality through experience and reflection.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It references fixed-trait models and the concept of traits being stable vs adaptable, which relates broadly to personality traits and their influence on behavior/performance.

options.content: It suggests personality is completely stable across the lifespan.
It suggests any apparent change is only due to short-term situational influences, not true trait change.
It shows personality can adapt over time through life experiences.
It suggests observed changes may reflect measurement error rather than actual changes in traits.

questions.specification: Why is it difficult to measure personality’s influence on performance?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks why measuring personality’s influence on performance is difficult, with the correct idea being interaction with environmental/situational factors—core to understanding personality traits and their relationship to behavior and performance.

options.content: The effects are often confounded by factors such as training history and motivation.
Personality traits are stable and can be measured directly with no measurement error.
It interacts with environment and situational factors.
Performance is influenced by multiple factors, so personality may show only weak or indirect relationships.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance

options.content: A stable characteristic pattern of behaving

options.content: Social learning theory
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this is a theoretical approach to how behaviour is learned, not a description/definition of personality shown here.

options.content: Environmental influence
options.markscheme: Incorrect: environmental factors can shape behaviour, but this option does not describe personality as a stable pattern/trait.

options.content: A short-term mood state
options.markscheme: Incorrect: mood is transient, whereas personality characteristics are relatively stable over time.

parts.content: Describe how trait theory explains individual differences in athletes.
parts.markscheme:
- Traits are stable and enduring characteristics <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: Explain how personality may influence an athlete’s choice of sport.
parts.markscheme:
- Extroverted individuals may prefer team sports due to social interaction and dynamic environments <marks>1</marks>
- Introverted athletes might choose individual sports where focus and independence are valued <marks>1</marks>
- Risk-taking personalities may be drawn to extreme or high-adrenaline sports (e.g., skiing, boxing) <marks>1</marks>
- Athletes with high conscientiousness may choose sports requiring discipline and routine (e.g., gymnastics, swimming) <marks>1</marks>

<note>
(Other acceptable answers: Emotional stability, openness to experience influencing sport type.)
</note>

[Max 4 marks]

parts.content: Outline the key features of the interactionist approach to personality.
parts.markscheme:
- Suggests personality is influenced by both traits (inherent characteristics) and situational factors <marks>1</marks>
- Emphasizes the dynamic interaction between the individual and the environment <marks>1</marks>
- Recognizes that behavior can change depending on the context (e.g., training vs. competition) <marks>1</marks>
- Allows for prediction of behavior by considering both personal disposition and situational cues <marks>1</marks>

<note>
(Other acceptable answers: Acknowledges variability in behavior across different sport scenarios.)
</note>

[Max 4 marks]

parts.content: Discuss how personality profiling may be used to enhance team selection and cohesion.

- Can prevent personality clashes <marks>1</marks>

questions.specification: Which statement best distinguishes personality traits from moods in sport?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to distinguish stable personality traits from temporary moods in sport, which directly falls under personality traits and their influence on thinking/behavior/performance.

options.content: Traits are relatively stable and long-term, while moods are temporary and fluctuate.
options.markscheme: Correct: traits are enduring dispositions, whereas moods are short-term affective states.

options.content: Traits are temporary and change rapidly, while moods are stable over time.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this reverses the typical distinction; traits are generally stable, moods are more transient.

options.content: Traits are relatively stable personal characteristics, while moods are situation-dependent, short-term states that can change during the day.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: although this describes traits and moods accurately, it does not distinguish them better than option A because it adds extra detail without changing the core distinction; A remains the best single answer.

options.content: Traits are learned only from experience, while moods are determined by genetics only.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: both traits and moods are influenced by multiple factors; neither is determined only by genetics or only by learning.

options.markscheme: Incorrect: changes in roles may influence behaviour in certain contexts, but this is less directly about enduring personality trait change than broader long-term experiences.

options.content: Daily mood swings
options.markscheme: Incorrect: mood is a short-term state and does not represent a change in stable personality traits.

options.content: Strictly due to inherited traits
options.markscheme: Incorrect: inherited factors contribute to personality, but personality is not determined strictly by heredity alone.

questions.specification: Which example reflects social learning theory in sports behaviour?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.2 Social learning theory and its role in shaping behavior through observation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.2 Social learning theory and its role in shaping behavior through observation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for an example of social learning theory in sport behaviour, which involves learning through observation and imitation (e.g., a gymnast copying an Olympic idol).

options.content: A young gymnast copying the routines of her Olympic idol
options.markscheme: Correct: demonstrates observational learning/modelling (imitation of a role model), consistent with social learning theory.

options.content: A sprinter inheriting fast-twitch muscle fibres
options.markscheme: Incorrect: reflects genetic/biological inheritance rather than learning through observation.

options.content: A basketball player continuing to train harder because praise from the coach follows extra effort
options.markscheme: Incorrect: reflects operant conditioning (reinforcement) rather than modelling/observational learning.

options.content: A tennis player persisting in practice because they feel autonomous and enjoy improving their skills
options.markscheme: Incorrect: reflects self-determination/intrinsic motivation rather than social learning via observation.

questions.specification: How does social learning theory explain aggressive behaviour in young athletes?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.2 Social learning theory and its role in shaping behavior through observation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.2 Social learning theory and its role in shaping behavior through observation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks how social learning theory explains aggressive behaviour in young athletes, which centers on observational learning/imitation of role models (as reflected in option B). This matches the leaf topic on social learning theory shaping behavior through observation.

options.content: Aggression is learned through reinforcement when aggressive acts are rewarded (for example, by praise or success).
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This describes reinforcement/operant conditioning rather than the key social learning mechanism of observational learning/modeling.

options.content: Children imitate aggressive behaviour observed in role models.
options.markscheme: Correct. Social learning theory proposes that aggression is learned through observational learning (modeling/imitation) of others such as parents, coaches, or elite athletes.

options.content: Aggression results mainly from frustration when goals are blocked during competition.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This aligns more with frustration–aggression explanations than social learning theory.

options.content: Aggression is primarily due to innate biological drives and is not influenced by observation of others.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Social learning theory emphasises environmental influence via observation and modeling, not innate drives as the primary cause.

options.markscheme: Aggressiveness can be considered a personality trait as it refers to a relatively stable characteristic of an individual across different situations.

options.markscheme: Calmness under stress can reflect emotional stability, which is a relatively stable personality trait.

options.content: Anxiety lasting only one game
options.markscheme: This describes state anxiety, which is a temporary emotional state triggered by a specific situation rather than a stable, enduring personality trait.

options.content: Discipline
options.markscheme: Discipline is associated with conscientiousness, which is one of the major dimensions of personality traits.

- Education <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Socioeconomic status <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Life experiences (eg major successes/failures, injury) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State two mechanisms by which an athlete’s personality can influence motivation.
parts.markscheme:
- Determines goal orientation (eg task vs ego) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Affects response to success and failure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Influences persistence/effort when training is difficult <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shapes reaction to feedback (eg motivated by praise vs specific corrective feedback) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Affects arousal/stress tolerance which can change willingness to compete <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how self-awareness supports athletic performance.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies strengths and weaknesses <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allows for targeted improvement (eg focusing practice on specific needs) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enhances control of emotional responses (eg recognising triggers and regulating arousal) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps set realistic goals and monitor progress <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improves decision-making during performance (eg recognising when concentration is dropping) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how feedback style should be adapted to different personalities.
parts.markscheme:
- Introverts may prefer private, detailed feedback <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Extroverts may enjoy verbal praise <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Athletes high in neuroticism may require feedback that is emotionally supportive and avoids harsh criticism to reduce anxiety <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Highly confident or assertive athletes may benefit from direct and challenging feedback that pushes them to improve <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Conscientious athletes usually appreciate structured, specific, and goal-oriented feedback they can act on immediately <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Feedback style influences motivation and learning <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Coaches must adapt based on athlete personality <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each point up to a maximum of <marks>4 marks</marks>.</note>

parts.content: Outline the importance of role models in shaping personality and mental approach.
parts.markscheme:
- Athletes emulate admired traits/behaviours <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Models influence effort, commitment and coping behaviours <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Role models shape mental attitudes toward success and failure (eg resilience after setbacks) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Role models can reinforce team norms/values and acceptable conduct <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Coaches can use appropriate models to guide behaviour (eg showcasing athletes who demonstrate desired mindset) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Negative role models may promote undesirable attitudes/behaviours <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how a coach can design the session structure and coaching climate to develop both skill and mental resilience.
parts.markscheme:
- Include appropriate challenge with support (progressive overload of technical/tactical demands while maintaining psychological safety) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use purposeful pressure (eg time constraints, scoring, competition in training) to practise coping skills <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Create a culture of accountability (clear expectations and consistent standards) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourage reflection and growth (eg debriefs, self-evaluation, goal review) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use failure as a learning tool (normalise mistakes and focus on controllable processes) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provide autonomy/supportive choices to increase ownership and intrinsic motivation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Balance feedback and reinforcement to maintain confidence while correcting errors <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which statement best reflects the idea of personality change?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.3 Long-term changes in personality through experience and reflection (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.3 Long-term changes in personality through experience and reflection (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about the concept of personality change over time (e.g., gradual long-term change), which directly matches long-term changes in personality through experience and reflection.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It broadly concerns personality (whether it is fixed or changeable), which relates to understanding personality traits and their general nature, though the primary focus is change over time.

options.content: Personality can change gradually over a long period of time.
options.markscheme: Correct. Personality traits can show gradual and systematic change over the lifespan (e.g., maturity principle).

options.content: Personality never changes after adolescence.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Longitudinal research indicates that personality traits can continue to change across adulthood.

options.content: Personality changes only due to short-term emotions.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Personality refers to relatively enduring traits, whereas short-term emotions are temporary states.

options.content: Personality changes rapidly and unpredictably from year to year for most adults.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. While change can occur, it is typically gradual and shows some stability rather than being rapid and unpredictable for most adults.

questions.specification: Which definition of personality is best reflected by describing an athlete as “introverted but highly disciplined”?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The description “introverted but highly disciplined” refers to a stable combination/pattern of personality traits, aligning with the topic on personality traits and their influence on behavior and performance.

options.content: Environmental conditioning only
options.markscheme: Personality is not explained solely by environmental conditioning; the description refers to enduring individual characteristics.

options.content: A pattern of stable personality traits
options.markscheme: “Introverted” and “highly disciplined” describe relatively stable traits; personality is commonly defined as a pattern of such traits.

options.content: A temporary psychological state
options.markscheme: A temporary state is short-lived (eg feeling anxious before a race), whereas introversion and discipline are enduring traits.

options.content: Social learning theory exclusively
options.markscheme: Social learning theory can contribute to personality development but does not exclusively define personality as described here.

questions.specification: Which of the following illustrates personality change over a career?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.3 Long-term changes in personality through experience and reflection (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.3 Long-term changes in personality through experience and reflection (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for an example of personality change over a career (e.g., nervous teenager becoming calm/confident as a professional), which aligns with long-term changes in personality through experience and reflection.

options.content: A teenager consistently nervous in competition becomes calm and confident as a professional.
options.markscheme: Correct. Personality refers to the relatively stable and enduring aspects of individuals. While traits are generally consistent, they can evolve over the long term; a shift from consistent nervousness to calm confidence over a career represents a change in these enduring personality traits.

options.content: A sprinter’s $100\text{ m}$ time improves after training.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This represents an improvement in physical performance and physiological adaptation (speed), not a change in personality.

options.content: A soccer player gains muscle mass in the off-season.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This is a physiological change (hypertrophy) related to physical conditioning and does not involve personality traits.

options.content: A gymnast feels nervous before one competition.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This is an example of state anxiety—a temporary emotional response to a specific situation—rather than a change in the enduring personality trait of trait anxiety.

questions.specification: Over many years, an athlete becomes more confident and outgoing. This supports the principle that:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.3 Long-term changes in personality through experience and reflection (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.3 Long-term changes in personality through experience and reflection (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario describes an athlete becoming more confident and outgoing over many years, indicating gradual, long-term personality change through experience.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.1 Individual differences > C.1.1 Personality > C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1.1 Personality traits and their influence on thinking, behavior, and performance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It broadly concerns personality traits (confidence, outgoingness) and how they relate to an individual over time, though the key emphasis is on long-term change.

options.content: Personality is fixed
Personality can change gradually over time
Only short-term emotions affect personality
Social learning theory alone explains personality
